Curriculum → High-level design interview prep → Application architecture and APIs → API design: REST, RPC, and contracts

Lesson 4 of 5

API design: REST, RPC, and contracts

Pick communication styles that match workflow complexity, evolution needs, and performance sensitivity.

25-35 min HLD flow Application architecture and APIs 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

The best API choice is the one that makes contracts understandable, evolvable, and efficient for the given clients.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. REST strengths HTTP semantics help with idempotency and caching2. RPC strengths Schema-first workflows improve consistency across teams3. Design contracts, not only endpoints Document retries and duplicate handling

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing The best API choice is the one that makes contracts understandable, evolvable, and efficient for the given clients. 2. REST strengths HTTP semantics help with idempotency and caching3. RPC strengths Schema-first workflows improve consistency across teams4. Design contracts, not only endpoints Document retries and duplicate handling
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

The best API choice is the one that makes contracts understandable, evolvable, and efficient for the given clients.

What to say first

  • Choose the API style based on clients and workload.
  • Discuss idempotency, pagination, and versioning.
  • Keep contracts explicit and evolution-friendly.
2

Study section

REST strengths

REST fits resource-oriented products, cacheable reads, and broad ecosystem compatibility. It works especially well when URLs and verbs map cleanly to product nouns and actions.

Call these points out explicitly

  • HTTP semantics help with idempotency and caching
  • Versioning and backward compatibility still require discipline
  • Payload size and over-fetching can matter on hot paths
3

Study section

RPC strengths

RPC is useful for strongly typed internal calls, explicit method contracts, and efficient binary serialization.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Schema-first workflows improve consistency across teams
  • Good for high-throughput internal service communication
  • You must still handle timeouts, retries, and versioning carefully
4

Study section

Design contracts, not only endpoints

State ownership, idempotency, pagination, error models, and auth scopes matter more than protocol fashion.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Document retries and duplicate handling
  • Keep backward-compatible changes easy
  • Return stable identifiers and machine-readable errors

Reference diagram

Visual anchor

RPC makes remote calls feel local, but the network semantics still matter.

Adapted from The System Design Primer by Donne Martin (CC BY 4.0).

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Choose the API style based on clients and workload.
  • Discuss idempotency, pagination, and versioning.
  • Keep contracts explicit and evolution-friendly.
  • Mention timeouts, retries, and error models.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Treating RPC as automatically faster without considering network behavior.
  • Designing REST APIs with action-heavy verbs but no resource model.
  • Ignoring compatibility once multiple clients exist.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • What makes an endpoint idempotent?
  • When would you choose RPC over REST for an internal platform?
  • How would you evolve an API used by mobile clients on old versions?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Real-time delivery: WebSockets, SSE, and long polling Application architecture and APIsQueues, streams, and background work Caching, asynchrony, and resilience

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: API design: REST, RPC, and contracts

Compare synchronous service hops against queued fan-out so you can explain where coupling shows up in production. Apply it to api design: rest, rpc, and contracts so the trade-offs feel concrete.

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Explain the concept

Teach "API design: REST, RPC, and contracts" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Guardrails

  • What makes an endpoint idempotent?
  • REST strengths
  • RPC strengths
  • Choose the API style based on clients and workload.

Suggested Structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model

Direct browser call to the Chat Completions API.
